## [00:00 - 03:03]

Alright, so for these next few clips, we're going to be going through pre-purchase flows. Again, we'll have pre-purchase and post-purchase, these next few starting with the welcome series are all for the pre-purchase. So the welcome series is by far, as I mentioned before, one of the most crucial series that you actually have to have built. With an average conversion rate of around 3%, a welcome series is one of the top-performing and highest-converting flows that you can have throughout your entire email stack. So someone's going to sign up on your website, whether it's through a pop-up, a fly-out, or an embedded form, and then they're going to expect to receive content from you either immediately or at least within the first hour. So let's take Nordstrom.com as a second example. You're landing on Nordstrom.com, you're not already a subscriber, you might get a welcome pop-up right upon load of the website, upon exiting the website, or you might scroll and see something embedded on their form that says, enter your email to get 10% off. You might also see something that says, enter your email to get free shipping, or you might see a dollar off coupon. This is what brands do to incentivize you to enter your email as a customer, and as a brand you have to leverage the content here to drive revenue and engagement for your subscribers. This is setting up your entire relationship with your subscribers. So the whole goal and point of the welcome series is to educate and nurture, and ideally you can convert people, but oftentimes you need multiple touch points with them, so these allow you to get multiple touch points with your customers. As mentioned with Nordstrom.com, the whole goal with them, as mentioned, is to educate you, set the expectation of what you're about to receive, and to give you the discount code, the coupon, or whatever it is that they promised in the pop-up. So for example, with Beardbrand, here's their first welcome email that you get, and this basically sets up the entire relationship and sets the expectations. So for example, at the top, you can see they say welcome to the Beardbrand. They have a really kind of warm and welcoming clip of their founding team and some other team members, and in the copy, you'll kind of see that they mention in the second line over the next 10 days that they'll be sending you content. So they're letting you know that this welcome series is going to last 10 days. That doesn't mean they're going to send you an email every single day. They might send you three emails over 10 days. They might send you five emails over 10 days. But what I'd normally recommend is sending between three to five emails over a week, a week and a half to two weeks, that gets your subscribers up to speed with what you're about and what you have to offer. If your brand is one that's fun and has a creative tone, it's really important in the welcome series to let this brand expression flow through. Don't be shy to have some comedy and some humor involved. It's really important that your subscribers understand you and get to know you for the brand and the values that you have. So for example, if you read their content, one of the lines is think of it as a beard mastery boot camp. And by the end of it, you'll be a master beardsman. So this copy is really fun and it kind of corresponds and correlates well to their audience. So don't be afraid to let this show through. In the welcome series, again, the whole goal is for you to educate your customers on who

## [03:03 - 06:07]

you are, why you matter, and how you're different. So a great way to do this is through building trust. So you can build trust through leveraging testimonials, places that you've been featured. So for example, I've been featured on CNBC or been featured in SQR Magazine, things like those all build trust. Having celebrity or influential partnerships are great ways to build trust. These are all things that help communicate that you're better than your competitor and hence why people should trust you and ultimately purchase from you instead. So for example, Tom Shoes is a great company that shows the impact right away. So for example, they've helped donate over a million pairs of shoes to people in need. This is a really cool stat that gets people excited about your brand, makes them want to purchase from you and shows that over a million other people have done the same. So a great way to show trust if you have a lot of customer reviews that are positive is to show the number of customer reviews you have. So for example, the brands we work have thousands, if not tens of thousands of positive reviews, normally over four and a half stars.
